topic: Introduction to Religions (Two from the SL list)
subject: religious-studies

items.q: What is the focus of virtue ethics?
items.correct: On character and habits — becoming the kind of person who acts well, aiming at eudaimonia (flourishing) through practising virtues that lie between extremes.
items.misconception: Virtue ethics tells you which specific actions are right and wrong.
items.random_wrong: Virtue ethics is about following the Ten Commandments.
items.why: Virtue ethics asks 'what kind of person should I be?' rather than 'what should I do?'. Students try to extract action-rules from it, missing its character-based agent-focused nature.

items.q: What does the Buddhist doctrine of anatta teach?
items.correct: There is no permanent, unchanging self or soul — what we call 'self' is a constantly changing flow of five aggregates (skandhas). This is distinctively Buddhist and differs from the Hindu atman.
items.misconception: Buddhists believe in a soul like the Christian or Hindu version.
items.random_wrong: Anatta means Buddhists have no personality.
items.why: Anatta is central to Buddhism and explicitly rejects the Hindu atman doctrine. Students assume all Eastern religions share the same soul concept, missing the deliberate contrast Buddhism draws.

items.q: Why do Jews not accept Jesus as the Messiah?
items.correct: Because the Jewish Messiah is expected to bring peace, rebuild the Temple and restore Israel — events the Jewish tradition holds Jesus did not accomplish.
items.misconception: Because Jews believe Jesus never existed.
items.random_wrong: Because Jesus was crucified, and the Messiah cannot die.
items.why: Most Jewish thinkers accept Jesus existed as a historical figure. The disagreement is about whether he fulfilled messianic prophecies — a theological judgement, not denial of history.

items.q: Whose Seven Dimensions framework analyses religion?
items.why: Correct answer: Smart. “Eliade” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's Durkheim. “Weber” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year did Max Muller help found comparative religion?
items.why: The real figure is 1870 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
